items.desc: Tiered SOC roles are blended to reduce handoffs; analysts are cross-trained to handle incidents end-to-end in a pod model.
states: Roles unclear; SOC tiers work in silos.
Skills matrix defined; cross-training plan in place.
Majority of analysts cross-trained; fewer handoffs across incident lifecycle.
Full pod model implemented for investigations; workload balancing largely automated.

items.desc: Dedicated ôbehind the glassö security engineering team exists to manage SOC tooling, content, and automation (platform integration, analytics tuning, etc.).
states: No dedicated security engineering function (reactive or ad-hoc only).
Part-time or ad-hoc engineering support assigned to the SOC.
Dedicated SecEng team in place managing integrations, detection content, etc.
Proactive SecEng function fully integrated; backlog handled with metrics-driven improvements.

items.desc: SOC analysts leverage generative AI (e.g. Security Copilot) to accelerate data gathering, triage, and investigation steps, appropriate to their roles (juniors speed up basics, seniors dive deeper faster). Post Ignite Æ25 insights:
states: No access to generative AI tools for SOC analysts.
Initial pilots of Copilot/GenAI assistants with a limited subset of analysts.
GenAI assistants broadly available; all analysts trained on AI-driven workflows.
GenAI embedded in daily operations for triage/investigation; measurable gains in analyst efficiency and insights.

items.desc: The organization develops new skills for managing AI-driven and autonomous operations û e.g. prompt engineering, agent orchestration, AI ethics and reliability oversight.
states: Necessary AI/automation skills are absent in the team.
Training plans established to build AI and autonomous operations skills.
Core team trained in AI governance, agent orchestration, and related skills.
AI and autonomy skills adopted SOC-wide; staff proficient in overseeing and co-working with AI agents.

items.desc: Key SOC processes (monitoring, investigation, response, etc.) are documented as SOPs with clear entry/exit criteria, roles (RACI), and escalation paths, and continuously refined.
states: Processes ad hoc and inconsistent; roles & handoffs unclear.
Core SOC procedures drafted and roles/responsibilities defined (SOPs in development).
Standard operating procedures approved and in use; workflows are clearly defined and enforced (predictable handoffs, RACI in place).
Processes routinely reviewed and optimized (metrics-driven improvements, minimal friction in workflows).

items.desc: The SOC actively monitors and reduces the organizationÆs attack surface (vulnerability management, cloud configuration, etc.), not just reacting to incidents.
states: Exposure management is purely reactive (patching after incidents).
Vulnerability scanning tools selected and basic remediation runbooks drafted.
Regular scans and attack surface reviews in place; remediation SLAs defined and tracked.
Continuous exposure management with risk-based prioritization of fixes; configuration drift and known weaknesses are promptly addressed.

items.desc: Analysts use natural-language interfaces and AI suggestions to handle incidents (e.g. ôexplain this alertö or timeline generation); prompt guides (prompt books) and verification steps ensure consistency.
states: No AI assistance in investigations or response (all steps manual).
Initial use of AI in investigations (some prompt patterns/assistants piloted).
Defined prompt playbooks and AI-driven suggestions integrated into standard operating procedures for triage/response.
Predominantly AI-driven workflows: analysts interact via Copilot/AI for most steps, with humans validating high-impact actions as needed.

items.desc: Autonomous ôsecurity agentö workflows execute complex tasks (threat hunts, incident response actions) based on goals, and improve over time by learning from outcomes (feedback loops, retained knowledge).
states: All investigation and response tasks executed manually (no autonomous workflows).
Candidate use cases for autonomous agents identified (design of agent workflows in progress).
Autonomous agent-driven workflows operating for specific tasks (goal-driven sequences with supervised self-evaluation).
Agents continuously learn from each engagement, improving efficacy (patterns reused, decisions refined) with minimal human intervention.

items.desc:
- XDR platforms can automatically contain threats: predictive shielding anticipates attacker moves and hardens targets; automatic attack disruption extends containment to third-party systems (e.g. AWS, Okta).
- Transforms IR from manual/reactive playbooks to proactive defense, with autonomous containment actions executed at machine speed.
states: All incident containment actions are manual (no automation).
Basic automated scripts for containment exist for a few scenarios (isolating a host, disabling an account) but usage is limited and requires human initiation.
Autonomous containment is enabled for common threats (e.g. auto-isolation of infected endpoints), including some third-party integrations; analysts supervise and fine-tune these automated actions

Predominantly AI-driven, preemptive containment in place û the SOCÆs XDR auto-hardens assets and disrupts attacks across on-prem and cloud with minimal human intervention (analysts oversee policies and intervene only when needed).

items.desc:
- The SOC extends into development: security tools (e.g. Defender for Cloud) integrate with code repositories and CI/CD pipelines to catch vulnerabilities early and tie them to runtime incidents for fast mitigation.
- Close collaboration with DevOps: code security findings (vulnerabilities, dependency risks) are handled as part of incident response, effectively ôshift-leftö û addressing threats in development to protect the software supply chain.
states: Development vulnerabilities are handled separately by developers (SOC not involved in code security).
Initial coordination between SOC and development teams established (e.g. sharing major vulnerability findings), but no formal integration; handling of code issues remains ad-hoc.

Security scanning in CI/CD pipelines is connected to SOC monitoring û critical code flaws trigger alerts to the SOC, and analysts work with DevOps on remediation; supply chain risks are getting incorporated into incident workflows.

Fully integrated DevSecOps workflow: code and pipeline security is a standard part of SOC operations. The SOC receives real-time alerts from development (vuln scans, repository changes) and uses AI-assisted tools to remediate issues quickly. ôShift-leftö is achieved û the SOC and DevOps function as one team on security,

items.desc: A unified security operations platform integrates XDR and SIEM capabilities, providing a single console for detection, investigation, and response across on-prem and cloud environments.
states: Disparate security tools (XDR, SIEM, etc.) with no unified interface.
Core XDR and SIEM platforms deployed; initial integration in progress.
Mostly unified SOC interface achieved (cross-product incident views and shared workspaces across tools).
Fully integrated ôsingle pane of glassö SOC platform (analysts navigate a seamless interface and can take cross-tool actions in one place).

items.desc:
- Visibility and security configuration management across multi-cloud assets (Azure, AWS, GCP, etc.) are unified in the SOC (XDR Dashboard), including serverless and cloud workloads.
- Up-to-date asset inventory and risk score across all environments.
-  Continuous monitoring of cloud app configurations (including serverless) as part of routine posture audits.
states: No centralized visibility into cloud asset posture (cloud security handled in silos).
Cloud asset inventory being planned; basic posture assessment processes defined.
Asset inventory and risk scoring in place for primary clouds; misconfigurations and weaknesses identified routinely.
Unified multi-cloud and even serverless posture management operational, with continuous monitoring and risk scoring of all cloud resources.

items.desc:
- Strong identity-focused security practices in place: enterprise-wide phishing-resistant MFA, user behavior analytics, and training to counter advanced phishing (including deepfakes).
- Even advanced attacks can be mitigated by MFA (blocking 99%+ of account takeover attempts).
states: Multi-factor authentication (MFA) not implemented (or only basic); phishing training ad-hoc.
Organization planning broad MFA rollout and formal phishing awareness training.
MFA enforced for most users; phishing defenses include user training and some anomaly detection (e.g. suspicious login alerts).
Phishing-resistant MFA deployed enterprise-wide; AI-driven detection of impersonation and other identity attacks actively protecting users.

items.desc:
- The SOC employs generative AI agents to sift logs, identify patterns, and assist in threat hunting and detection engineering (e.g. suggesting queries or automating hypotheses generation).
- Natural language ôThreat-Hunting Agentsö and Copilot-driven workflows.
- Defined procedures for validating AI outputs
- Agents integrated into daily triage and hunting.
states: No AI assistance in log analysis or threat hunting (fully manual).
Pilot projects using AI for basic alert triage or log analysis in development.
Copilot/AI agents being rolled out to aid threat hunting and analysis (e.g. automated log summarization, anomaly spotting).
AI agents are fully integrated into detection and hunting workflows; even non-expert analysts launch complex hunts via natural language queries.

items.desc: Comprehensive telemetry collection across endpoints, identities, email, network, and cloud (and OT/ICS where applicable) to ensure no blind spots in detection.
states: Only minimal security logs/sources are collected.
Coverage plan established to onboard all critical telemetry sources.
~80û90% of required data sources sending telemetry; remaining gaps identified and tracked.
>90% of environments covered by telemetry; any gaps are automatically flagged for remediation.

items.desc: Security data is normalized to common schemas and correlated, enabling entity-centric and incident-centric views with context (asset info, threat intel) attached to alerts.
states: Logs are siloed and unnormalized (raw data only, difficult to cross-correlate).
Basic normalization in place for some data sources.
Unified schema adopted; events from multiple sources are correlated into incidents and entity (asset/user) profiles.
Richly correlated incidents with entity graphs and context (threat intel enrichment, etc.) provide high-fidelity alerts.

items.desc:
- Threat intelligence feeds (IOCs, actor TTPs) are integrated, and the SOC tracks adversary campaigns. Intel is applied to detect and proactively hunt threats, including those emerging on the dark web. 
- Behavior-based detection and dark web monitoring to counter increasingly AI-augmented, credential-focused attacks).
states: No threat intelligence integrated into SOC monitoring.
One threat feed/source piloted in SOC tools.
Multiple threat intel sources actively integrated (indicators matched, actor profiles available).
Threat intel fully operationalized û feeds from many sources (incl. dark web) inform detections and hunting; adversary campaigns are tracked and used to adapt defenses.

items.desc: The SOC captures investigation context, summaries, and rationales in case records. This knowledge is indexed for future retrieval, aiding in learning from past incidents.
states: Lessons and investigation details are not systematically captured.
Templates defined for capturing key findings and decisions in incident reports.
Investigation summaries and analyst reasoning are automatically captured for each case.
Past case knowledge is leveraged in new investigations (similar cases retrieved with context; ôinstitutional memoryö informs current ops).

items.desc: Machine learning models continuously analyze telemetry to detect anomalies across users, endpoints, and environments; models retrain and improve as new data comes in.
states: No machine-learning-based anomaly detection on SOC data.
Plan in development to deploy anomaly detection pipelines (ML models) on security data.
Initial ML jobs running, detecting anomalies across entities and environments (outliers flagged for review).
Continuous ML analytics tuned and improving over time (drift monitored; models adapt to new patterns to reduce false negatives).

items.desc:
- Cybersecurity is managed as a business risk: regular board/executive oversight, tracked via key metrics (e.g. MFA coverage, patch times) and aligned with major frameworks/regulations.
- Compliance with frameworks (e.g. NIST CSF 2.0) and regulations (e.g. EU AI Act). 
-Senior leadership briefed on SOC metrics and alignment with new and updated standards.
- AI risk management integrated into GRC charters, procedures, and processes.
- Regulatory scrutiny extended to AI use in security operations.
states: Cyber risk is not discussed at board level; minimal executive reporting.
Planning to report cybersecurity posture to executives and align with emerging standards.
Standardized cyber metrics (MFA coverage, IR time, etc.) reported to leadership; partial adoption of new frameworks.
Full integration of cyber risk into governance (board-level dashboards, compliance with NIST CSF 2.0, EU AI Act, etc.; AI risk management included in charters).

items.desc: Day-to-day SOC operations have governance controls: changes to detection content or playbooks follow change control, and evidence from investigations is captured for audits and compliance.
states: No formal change control for SOC content; evidence collection ad hoc.
Change management process defined (CAB for analytics/automation changes) and evidence templates introduced.
Change control enforced for SOC updates (approval gates for rules, runbooks) and investigation evidence captured by default.
Fully auditable operations: change decisions metrics-driven (with rollback plans); investigation evidence and activity logs automatically collected and auditable.

items.desc: Policies and guardrails are in place for SOC use of generative AI (e.g. Security Copilot): prompt content is reviewed, outputs are logged and validated, and human oversight is mandated for high-impact decisions.
states: No specific policies or logging for AI assistance usage in the SOC.
Draft AI usage policy in place (e.g. guidelines for Copilot prompts); partial logging of AI outputs.
GenAI usage is governed with defined approval checkpoints and full logging of all prompts and AI-generated content.
Regular audits of AI assistance outputs and decisions; prompt policies and validation checks are continuously refined based on oversight findings.

items.desc:
- Autonomous AI agents (for security or other business functions) are treated as managed identities (through Entra Agent ID): each agent is inventoried, given appropriate access, and subject to lifecycle controls and monitoring.
- Agent ID supplemented with broad-scale agent security and governance through Agent 365 (Agent ID-based, non-Agent ID-based or shadow)
states: No framework to govern AI agents; any bots/agents operate without oversight.
Inventory of AI agents and their access is being planned (defining ownership and controls).
Rolling out Entra Agent ID or equivalent: each in-house AI agent registered, with access policies and lifecycle (update/retirement) procedures.

Full governance in place for AI agents as first-class identities û their accounts, permissions, and activities are managed and reviewed like those of human admins (with compliance and security controls).

items.desc:
- Robust incident response extends to recovery: disaster recovery (DR) plans, backups, and business continuity are well-established and regularly drilled.
- Continued regular disaster recovery drills and tabletop exercises ensure the SOC can recover from major incidents. 
- Recovery plans adjusted/continuously improved relative to ongoing advances in AI.
- Investment for improved resilience in the face of novel attack scenarios.
states: No formal disaster recovery (DR) or business continuity plans in place.
DR plan development underway; initial tabletop incident recovery exercises planned.
Standard DR/BC procedures documented and tested regularly (e.g. annual DR drills).
Mature resilience program with continuous improvement; sophisticated scenarios (including AI-driven incidents) are tested to validate recovery capabilities.

items.desc: The SOC has a defined catalog of services it provides (monitoring, threat hunting, incident response, etc.), with associated SLAs/OLAs for internal customers.
states: No formal definition of SOC services (capabilities not clearly communicated).
Draft SOC service catalog created (key services identified with basic descriptions).
Service catalog published, including SLAs/OLAs for services (e.g. incident response times).
Service offerings reviewed regularly (e.g. quarterly) and adjusted based on usage and feedback; service performance (uptake, timeliness) is tracked.

items.desc: The SOC operating model (staffing, shifts, on-call rotations, handoff to incident management or crisis teams) is clearly defined and practiced, including surge support procedures.
states: Roles/responsibilities and handoff points in SOC operations are unclear.
Staffing model and on-call rotations drafted (roles and escalation paths defined on paper).
Roster and shift schedule implemented; handoffs to incident management/crisis teams are predictable and smooth.
Well-tuned operating model with data-driven adjustments (e.g. staffing analytics, proven surge playbooks for major incidents).

items.desc: A structured program exists to adopt Copilot/AI in SOC workflows (use-case selection, training for personnel) alongside defined norms for when to trust AI output vs. escalate to humans.
states: No formal plan for integrating AI tools into SOC processes.
Roadmap created for Copilot/AI adoption; initial training and AI usage guidelines drafted for SOC staff.
Active AI adoption program: analysts trained by role; clear norms established for how AI suggestions are verified or escalated.
Benefits of AI integration are tracked and the program is expanded; AI usage policies are continuously calibrated based on outcomes and user feedback.

items.desc:
- The SOC has a roadmap and procedures for increasing autonomous operations: e.g. defining agent Service Level Objectives (SLOs), assigning ôowner of recordö for agent-driven incidents, and criteria for phasing out manual tasks.
- Human and machine oversight of AI agents performing security or business tasks.
- Piloting of small-scale autonomous tasks (like automated recon or ticket handling) in the SOC.
- Clear guardrails and fallback to human control, to build trust in agent-driven operations gradually.
states: No plan or model for introducing autonomous agents into SOC processes.
Initial roadmap for autonomous SOC operations drafted (includes agent SLOs and roles for oversight).
Partial autonomy in place: agent-driven tasks have SLOs and owners; roadmap execution (phasing in agents for specific use cases) underway.

Autonomous SOC model fully implemented: agents carry out a growing range of tasks per roadmap; agent performance meets SLOs, and manual steps are retired according to policy (with human oversight checkpoints maintained).

items.desc: Key efficiency metrics like Mean-Time-to-Detect and Respond (MTTD, MTTR), false positive rates, etc., are measured to gauge SOC performance.
states: No tracking of basic detection/response timing or accuracy.
Baseline detection and response times measured.
Targets set for MTTD, MTTR, false-positive rate, etc., and metrics reported regularly.
Detection and response speed targets consistently met; continuous improvement process in place (e.g. tuning to further reduce MTTR).

items.desc: Metrics cover the completeness and quality of monitoring and controls (e.g. data source health, logging coverage, control efficacy, configuration drift).
states: Unknown coverage/health of logging and controls.
Health checks of data sources and control configurations piloted.
Telemetry and control health is tracked (gaps and misconfigurations identified and remediated).
Automated alerts for data/control failures; configuration drift or coverage gaps are prevented or corrected in near-real time.

items.desc: Security outcomes are tied to business metrics û e.g. breach impact, incident costs, and dwell time û and reviewed with business stakeholders to ensure SOC value delivery.
states: SOC metrics not linked to business outcomes (e.g. financial or operational impact).
Initial mapping of SOC performance to business KPIs (e.g. downtime, financial impact of incidents).
Business impact of security incidents reviewed periodically (e.g. quarterly reports on incident cost, downtime, etc.).
Clear improvements in business risk reduction (incident dwell time down, cost per incident down, and fewer business-disrupting incidents).

items.desc: Metrics track analyst productivity and consistency (e.g. cases handled per analyst, time saved via automation, quality of documentation) to identify training needs and improvements.
states: No measurements of individual analyst workload or work quality.
Metrics defined for analyst performance (e.g. average handling time, investigation thoroughness) and baseline measured.
Regular reporting on analyst productivity and quality (e.g. monthly case audits, peer reviews); improvements initiatives in place.
Noticeable increase in analyst throughput with maintained/improved quality (performance targets met; variance between analysts reduced through best practices).

items.desc: New metrics assess the impact of automation/AI: percentage of incidents handled autonomously vs. with human input, agent error rates or rollbacks, and the shift in human workloads (toward proactive vs. reactive work).
states: No tracking of the effect of AI/autonomy on SOC operations.
Baseline established for automation levels and agent error rates (if any).
Trends show increasing automation with acceptable error rates; some analyst capacity freed for higher-value tasks (tracked qualitatively).
Quantitative targets for autonomy reached (e.g. X% of alerts auto-handled) with minimal safety incidents; analystsÆ focus has shifted to strategic tasks and overall cost per incident is reduced.
